title: Why the return of boundaries makes some relationships impossible
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:

 It's subtle at first. You say no to a phone call because you're exhausted. You tell your partne...
htmlContent:
You start setting boundaries and the whole world shifts

It's subtle at first. You say no to a phone call because you're exhausted. You tell your partner you need an hour alone after work. You decline an invitation from a friend who always drains you. And you feel a flicker of something unfamiliar. Relief. Maybe even pride.

Then the phone goes quiet. The texts stop. The invitations dry up. And you're left wondering if you did something wrong.

Here's the thing. You didn't do anything wrong. You did something right. But the people around you weren't ready for it. And some of them never will be.

When you've spent years, decades even, being the person who absorbs others' emotions, who says yes when you mean no, who contorts yourself into whatever shape keeps the peace, your boundaries don't just inconvenience people. They threaten them. They threaten the entire system that was built around your lack of boundaries.

Bessel van der Kolk, the trauma researcher who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, talks about how trauma lives in the body and in our relationships. When you start setting boundaries, you're not just changing a behavior. You're changing the relational patterns that have been wired into your nervous system since childhood. And the people around you feel that shift. They feel it in their own nervous systems. And they don't like it.

Think about it. Your mother who calls you three times a day. Your friend who unloads every crisis on you without asking how you are. Your boss who expects you to answer emails at 10 PM. These relationships were built on a specific dynamic. You gave. They took. You absorbed. They discharged. And now you're saying, "I can't do this anymore."

That's not a boundary. That's a betrayal. At least that's how it feels to them.

The nervous system doesn't negotiate

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us that our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. When you've been in a relationship where you're the one who regulates everyone else's emotions, your nervous system learns that your safety depends on keeping others calm. You learn to override your own signals. You learn to ignore the tightness in your chest, the knot in your stomach, the voice that says "I don't want to do this."

Setting a boundary is an act of nervous system rebellion. You're telling your body, "I'm going to listen to you now, even if it costs me this relationship."

And here's what happens. The person on the other side of that boundary doesn't have the capacity to handle it. Their nervous system has been conditioned to rely on your availability. When you withdraw, they feel abandoned. They feel rejected. They feel attacked. And they respond the only way they know how. They escalate. They guilt-trip. They withdraw. They punish you for taking care of yourself.

Stay with me here. This is the part that hurts. Some relationships simply cannot survive your healing. Not because you're doing it wrong. Not because you're being too harsh or too rigid. But because the relationship was built on a foundation that required you to be small, compliant, and available at all times.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker, the author, describes exactly this dynamic. The way we learn to fawn and appease to stay safe. And the terror that comes when we stop.

The ones who can't meet you where you are

I've watched this play out in my own life. There was a friend I'd known for fifteen years. We'd been through breakups, job losses, family crises together. I was always the one who listened. The one who dropped everything to be there. The one who absorbed her anxiety and sent back reassurance.

When I started setting boundaries, she didn't know what to do with me. I told her I couldn't talk after 9 PM because I needed to sleep. She said I was being cold. I told her I couldn't be her only support system anymore. She said I was abandoning her. I told her I needed to focus on my own healing. She said I'd changed and not for the better.

She was right about one thing. I had changed. I was no longer willing to be the emotional garbage disposal for everyone in my life. And that change made our relationship impossible.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who writes about structural dissociation, talks about how parts of us get frozen in time. We have the part that learned to survive by taking care of others. And when that part starts to heal, the people who depended on that part feel like they're losing something. Because they are. They're losing access to your caretaking energy. And some of them will do anything to get it back.

They'll tell you that you're being selfish. That you've changed. That you're not the person they used to know. And you know what? They're not entirely wrong. You're not that person anymore. And that's the whole point.

Grieving what you never really had

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). Lindsay C. Gibson writes about how children of emotionally immature parents learn to become the caretakers. They learn to suppress their own needs to keep the peace. And then they carry that pattern into every relationship they form as adults.

So when you start setting boundaries, you're not just losing relationships. You're losing the illusion that those relationships were ever truly mutual. You're grieving the fantasy that if you just tried harder, gave more, loved better, they would finally see you and love you back the way you needed.

That grief is real. And it's necessary. But it's also disorienting. Because you're not just losing people. You're losing the version of yourself that existed within those relationships. The people-pleaser. The fixer. The one who could tolerate anything because she didn't believe she deserved better.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman, who wrote it, describes how trauma survivors often need to rebuild their capacity for relationships from the ground up. That means letting go of relationships that can't hold the new you. And that is one of the hardest things you'll ever do.

The empty space is not a void

Here's what nobody tells you about boundaries. When you clear out the people who couldn't handle them, you're left with silence. And that silence can feel terrifying at first. Your nervous system has been conditioned to expect chaos, obligation, and the constant hum of other people's needs. Without that noise, you might feel like you're disappearing.

But you're not disappearing. You're arriving. You're finally in the room with yourself. And that's a relationship you've been avoiding your whole life.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems, explains that every part of you, even the ones that seem dysfunctional, has a positive intention. The part that kept you in bad relationships was trying to protect you. The part that couldn't say no was trying to keep you safe. And now that part needs to learn a new job. It needs to learn that you can survive without those relationships.

Wild, right? The thing that kept you safe is now the thing that needs to heal.

So you sit in the quiet. You feel the discomfort. You notice the urge to reach out, to apologize, to take back your boundary and make everything okay again. And you don't. You stay. You let the discomfort be there without fixing it. And slowly, something shifts. The quiet starts to feel less like emptiness and more like space. Space to breathe. Space to feel what you actually feel. Space to discover what you want instead of what everyone else needs from you.

Not everyone will come with you

I wish I could tell you that everyone who matters will stay. That the people who love you will rise to meet your boundaries and celebrate your growth. Some will. Some absolutely will. And those relationships will become deeper and more authentic than anything you've ever known.

But some won't. And that's not a reflection of your worth or your healing. It's a reflection of their capacity. Some people simply cannot be in a relationship with someone who has boundaries because it forces them to confront their own lack of boundaries. It holds up a mirror to their own avoidance. And they'd rather break the mirror than look at themselves.

That's not your fault. And it's not your responsibility to fix.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma recovery is a process of titration. You don't dump the whole story at once. You go slowly. You touch the edge of the wound and then you come back to safety. Boundaries work the same way. You set one. You see what happens. You adjust. You set another. And over time, you build a new map of what relationships can look like.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. The tightness in your chest. The knot in your stomach. The voice that says "but what if I'm being too harsh?" That's the part of you that's still afraid of being abandoned. That's the part that needs reassurance that you're not a bad person for taking care of yourself.

You're not a bad person. You're a person who's finally choosing herself. And that's allowed.

Some relationships will survive this shift. Some will transform into something better. And some will end. And that ending, as painful as it is, is not a failure. It's a clearing. It's the space being made for relationships that can actually hold you, all of you, including your boundaries.

Sit with that for a moment. The relationships that can't hold your boundaries were never holding you anyway. They were holding a version of you that was slowly dying inside. Letting them go isn't losing something real. It's finally telling the truth about what was never real in the first place.

The return of boundaries makes some relationships impossible. And that's exactly how it should be.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link)
